feature_name: Dual-mode operation (Root Complex or End Point)
description: The controller supports operation as either a PCIe Root Complex or a PCIe End Point. Role selection is driven by bootstrap pins and can be overridden in device status registers before training. In RC mode it initiates configuration cycles and manages downstream enumeration for attached endpoints. In EP mode it exposes Type 0 configuration space and responds to host configuration requests. The selected role affects availability of certain apertures and the behavior of configuration and translation registers.
dependencies: PSC power enabled; DEVSTAT[PCIESSEN]=1; valid SerDes refclk; finalize role before enabling LTSSM
Remarks: Select the intended role early at boot and keep it constant until a full reset and retrain.

description: The link supports 2.5 GT/s (Gen1) and 5.0 GT/s (Gen2) operational rates. Negotiation occurs during training under software policy via Link Control 2 and Port Logic GEN2 controls. Gen2 stability requires correct PHY equalization, board design, and clocking alignment. Software should bring up initially at Gen1, verify stability, then request Gen2 if supported. Always verify the negotiated speed post-training before enabling high-throughput features.
evidence: LINK_CTRL2; Port Logic GEN2 registers (PL_GEN2); LINK_STAT_CTRL
dependencies: Stable refclk; SerDes PLL lock; correct COMMON_CLK/SSC settings; proper routing
Remarks: Gate Gen2 enable behind a proven link to minimize bring-up instability.

feature_name: Link width x1/x2 with lane reversal and polarity inversion
description: The controller operates with x1 or x2 lanes depending on platform routing. Automatic lane reversal and receive polarity inversion enable flexible PCB routing without manual remaps. Down-training allows operation when only a subset of lanes are detected. Status registers expose active lanes and alignment to confirm negotiated width. Software should avoid forcing overrides unless board constraints demand it.
evidence: PCS_STATUS lane info; Port Logic/Debug lane state; SERDES_CFG0/1
dependencies: SerDes PLL locked; intended lanes enabled; partner device compatible
Remarks: Prefer automatic negotiation and use manual constraints only when necessary.

description: Outbound translation maps SoC addresses into PCIe memory, I/O, or configuration spaces. Windows are programmed via indexed offset and size registers and then collectively enabled. Correct ordering requires programming target base and size prior to enabling the feature. Software must avoid generating outbound TLPs until link is up to prevent timeouts. Misconfiguration can cause decode errors or unintended remote accesses.
dependencies: Program base/size first; enable after configuration; require link L0 before use
Remarks: Guard all outbound accesses with link-up checks and target presence verification.

description: Inbound translation exposes internal memory regions to the PCIe fabric. Regions are tied to BAR selection, start, and offset registers and must match host expectations in EP mode. Enable inbound translation only after BAR apertures are negotiated by the host. Sizes and alignments should be minimized to reduce attack surface and complexity. Proper configuration allows peer and host-initiated writes to internal memory.
dependencies: BARs sized and negotiated; translators enabled; non-cacheable attributes set
Remarks: Keep apertures as small as practical while meeting performance needs.

description: Application endianness for internal interfaces is software selectable. Configuration must match the system architecture to avoid misinterpretation of descriptors and data. This must be set before enabling translators or starting DMA/CPU traffic. Changes after traffic begins require quiescence and revalidation to ensure integrity. Host-visible register layouts must be audited for cross-endian correctness.
dependencies: Set prior to OB/IB enable and any DMA or CPU MMIO transactions
Remarks: Document platform endianness and validate all host-visible fields for consistency.

feature_name: TLP attributes: Relaxed Ordering and No Snoop
description: Outbound TLPs can be tagged with Relaxed Ordering and No Snoop attributes. These settings may improve throughput on systems tolerant of reordering and without snoop-coherent paths. Some partners ignore or restrict these attributes, so interoperability must be validated. Use only when memory consistency requirements permit and measure system impact. Start conservatively and enable selectively for large sequential transfers.
dependencies: Interconnect tolerates reordering; cache policy permits no-snoop; performance validated
Remarks: Apply attributes only to flows proven safe to reorder or bypass snoop.

description: Legacy INTA/B/C/D signaling is supported and mapped to SoC interrupt inputs. Correct operation requires enabling masks, servicing status, and issuing EOI writes. Latched status typically uses write-one-to-clear semantics. Legacy interrupts are useful for compatibility and early bring-up. Under sustained load, MSI usually offers lower latency and better scalability.
dependencies: SoC interrupt routing configured; vectors mapped; W1C semantics followed
Remarks: Prefer MSI when available; retain INTx for fallback scenarios.

description: In Root Complex mode the controller receives and services MSIs from endpoints. MSI vectors are grouped across enable and status blocks with W1C semantics. Software should enable only required vectors and coalesce handling to reduce overhead. Ensure endpoints are configured for MSI during enumeration for best performance. Proper EOI handling is required to complete the interrupt service path.
dependencies: Enumeration enables endpoint MSI; SoC IRQ mapping done; status/EOI ordering respected
Remarks: Batch process vectors per group to minimize MMIO traffic at high rates.

description: In End Point mode the controller can generate MSI to signal events to the host. Software programs the local MSI capability and writes the trigger to raise interrupts. Host enumeration must allocate, map, and enable vectors for proper operation. Ordering must ensure that data is visible before signaling the host. MSI should be preferred over legacy INTx when supported for lower overhead.
evidence: Local MSI capability in config space; MSI trigger via MSI_IRQ (0x54)
dependencies: MSI capability configured; host vectors assigned and enabled; valid BAR/IB regions
Remarks: Coalesce interrupts carefully to balance latency and CPU overhead.

description: The module supports device power management via standard capabilities. Active State Power Management enables L0s and L1 link states for energy savings. Entry and exit should be coordinated with quiesced traffic to prevent data loss. Latency impacts must be considered for real-time or low-latency paths. The L2 link state is not supported and should not be used in policy decisions.
dependencies: Stable link; partner ASPM support; quiesce traffic before transitions
Remarks: Enable ASPM only after validating platform clocking and latency budgets.

description: Dedicated interrupts report power management and reset-related events. Software must enable, service, and clear both raw and masked status. Proper ordering captures root causes before clearing to avoid losing diagnostic context. These events coordinate orderly shutdowns and recoveries. Neglecting them can stall transitions or leave stale states latched.
dependencies: SoC IRQ mapping; PM sequences implemented; respect status clear ordering
Remarks: Persist PM/reset causes to logs for field diagnostics and policy tuning.

description: Advanced Error Reporting extends visibility into correctable and uncorrectable errors. End-to-end CRC generation and checking detect TLP corruption across the fabric. Software should enable error logging and thresholds aligned with reliability goals. On error, capture and persist logs before clearing to preserve root cause. Interoperability testing is advised since some partners do not fully implement AER.
dependencies: AER capability present; interrupts routed; sufficient buffers for logs
Remarks: Integrate counters into health monitoring and trigger recovery on fatal errors.

description: The PHY requires programming of SerDes PLL controls followed by lock verification. Writes may require a device-specific unlock sequence using a kicker mechanism. After enabling the PLL, specified wait times must be observed before assuming stability. Software must poll the lock bit and only proceed when asserted. Lane-specific status aids diagnosis of signal integrity or configuration issues.
dependencies: Stable refclk applied; kicker unlocked; observe post-enable delays
Remarks: Abstract SerDes programming into a reusable routine across KeyStone variants.

feature_name: Link training control and LTSSM monitoring
description: Software enables the Link Training and Status State Machine to initiate negotiation. Training should remain disabled until translators and configuration are safe. After enabling, status registers reveal the LTSSM state with L0 indicating an operational link. Failure to reach L0 often indicates clocking, cabling, or configuration issues. Automated bring-up should gate enumeration on successful L0 detection with bounded retries.
dependencies: SerDes lock achieved; translators configured; link event interrupts routed
Remarks: Implement timeouts and retries to handle marginal links gracefully.

description: Software can issue a downstream hot reset to recover faulty endpoints. The reset command is write-one-to-set and requires traffic to be flushed beforehand. Adequate delays must be honored before resuming transactions to allow reinitialization. Proper use enables clean re-enumeration of devices after faults. Misuse can lead to timeouts or prolonged link hangs.
evidence: RSTCMD[INIT_RST] (0x14); RSTCMD[FLUSH_N] semantics; timing guidance (>=100 ms)
dependencies: RC role; traffic quiesced; timers enforced before access
Remarks: Incorporate hot reset into layered recovery with exponential backoff.

description: In RC mode the controller issues configuration and I/O cycles through dedicated outbound windows. Software selects Type 0 or Type 1 targets via the setup register. Accesses must be single 32-bit, non-burst transactions to remain within controller rules. Attempts while the link is down or with no target present can hang the bus. Guard all operations with link and presence checks.
evidence: CFG_SETUP (0x08); IOBASE (0x0C, 4 KB window); non-burst rule
dependencies: Link trained to L0; targets discovered; windows configured before use
Remarks: Prefer memory space over I/O for modern endpoints when available.

description: Inbound and outbound translators are gated by centralized enable bits. This allows staged configuration and quick isolation during debug. Proper sequencing reduces exposure to malformed or misrouted TLPs. Translators should be disabled before performing major remaps to avoid stray accesses. Centralized control simplifies feature flag management across the driver.
dependencies: OB/IB tables valid; known link state; handlers ready
Remarks: Provide a single control point for translator state in the driver framework.

description: Per-lane PCS and SerDes configuration and status are available in application space. These registers enable fine-grained debug, resets, and margining during SI bring-up. They expose lane enable states and readiness for validation. Powerful controls should not be altered in production without extensive testing. Accesses must still adhere to strict MMIO rules to avoid perturbations.
dependencies: SerDes powered; kicker unlocked if required; expert-only usage
Remarks: Hide expert knobs behind guarded build flags and service interfaces.

description: The module interfaces to the SoC with wide internal buses to support high throughput. Controller special address spaces require single 32-bit, non-burst, non-cacheable accesses. Violating these rules can cause protocol errors or deadlocks within the controller. DMA paths should use memory-mapped windows and avoid special controller spaces. Centralized MMIO helpers enforce correct width and ordering semantics.
evidence: Functional overview; Access rules for app/local/remote config/IO spaces
dependencies: MMIO attributes set to device/strongly-ordered; driver enforces access width
Remarks: Use dedicated helpers to prevent accidental wide or burst accesses.

description: When a common reference clock is shared with the link partner, software must set the common-clock bit. Spread-spectrum clocking reduces EMI but requires matching settings on both ends. Mismatched policies can degrade link stability, especially at Gen2 speeds. Proper configuration maximizes interoperability and reliability. Always confirm negotiated settings in link status registers post-training.
evidence: LINK_STAT_CTRL[COMMON_CLK_CFG]; PL_GEN2 notes on Tx swing and SSC
dependencies: Knowledge of board clock topology; stable refclk; SerDes configured accordingly
Remarks: Document platform clock topology and validate during manufacturing tests.

description: A structured sequence transitions the controller to operational RC state. Power and configure SerDes before enabling training to ensure signal integrity. Program endianness, translators, and interrupts with LTSSM disabled to avoid premature traffic. Enable LTSSM, wait for L0, then enumerate devices and enable MSI where supported. Include bounded timeouts and retries to accommodate slow or marginal devices.
dependencies: PSC power; SerDes lock; translators configured; IRQ routing; enumeration code
Remarks: Automate with checkpoints and clear error handling for robust bring-up.

description: End Point bring-up configures BARs, capabilities, and inbound windows prior to training. SerDes must be configured and locked before enabling LTSSM for link negotiation. After link-up, the host performs configuration cycles which must be serviced reliably. MSI should be enabled and mapped when supported to reduce interrupt latency. Defer DMA until host completes configuration and validates memory windows.
dependencies: BAR sizes chosen; IB/OB programmed; SerDes lock; LTSSM gating
Remarks: Follow host assignment precisely to avoid enumeration conflicts.

description: The module does not provide hardware hot-plug support and does not support the L2 link state. Software must avoid advertising or relying on these capabilities during enumeration or power management. Power policies should target L0s/L1 states only for energy savings. Recovery flows should not assume L2 transitions are available. Documentation should reflect these architectural limitations for platform designers.
evidence: Unsupported features list (no L2, no HW hot-plug)
dependencies: System design avoids L2/hot-plug dependencies; PM policies bounded to L0s/L1
Remarks: Use platform-level solutions for presence changes if hot-plug is required.

feature_name: Payload and read request size limits
description: The controller enforces maximum payload and request sizes in each direction. Outbound payload is limited to 128 bytes while inbound payloads and MRRS allow up to 256 bytes. Enumeration software must program matching values in capability registers. Descriptor sizing and batching should align with these negotiated limits. Improper settings can reduce performance or cause protocol rejections by peers.
dependencies: Enumeration negotiates MPS/MRRS within limits; endpoints configured accordingly
Remarks: Tune DMA chunking to align with MPS/MRRS and IOMMU boundaries.

description: Inbound transactions can carry priority and privilege identifiers for system QoS integration. Software should set conservative defaults to prevent starvation or unfairness. Policies must align with SoC fabric rules and, in safety systems, be audited. Misconfiguration can overload or starve other masters on the interconnect. Runtime tunability can be exposed cautiously for performance tuning.
dependencies: Coordination with SoC QoS/security frameworks; policy defined and reviewed
Remarks: Expose tunables via platform configuration with safe defaults.

description: Activity status and diagnostic controls provide operational insight. They can indicate ongoing transactions, internal errors, or throttling states. Overuse of intrusive diagnostics may perturb timing and traffic. Access must follow non-burst MMIO rules and be rate-limited in production. Periodic sampling should be correlated with interrupt and performance logs for actionable insights.
dependencies: Use non-burst MMIO helpers; limit access frequency in production
Remarks: Enable detailed diagnostics only in service or lab builds.

description: A small I/O space window is provided for legacy device access in RC mode. Many modern endpoints do not implement I/O and prefer memory-mapped access. Accesses must be single 32-bit, non-burst transactions to remain within controller rules. Software should validate device capabilities before relying on I/O space. Prefer memory space where possible to improve performance and compatibility.
dependencies: RC mode; endpoints that advertise I/O BARs only
Remarks: Fallback to memory space if I/O BARs are absent or problematic.

description: Multiple virtual channels or traffic classes beyond defaults are not supported by this controller. Function-level reset is also not implemented and must not be advertised. Enumeration must reflect these limits to avoid interoperability issues. Attempting to enable unsupported features can lead to link instability or configuration errors. Keep capability advertisement minimal for broad compatibility across partners.
evidence: Unsupported features list (no multi-VC/TC; no FLR)
dependencies: Enumeration logic constrained to supported capabilities only
Remarks: Document limits clearly to avoid incorrect feature negotiation.
